sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Researchers treated isolated mitochondria with three compounds while measuring oxygen consumption and ATP production. Compound A blocked Complex IV. Compound B uncoupled proton movement from ATP synthesis by carrying protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane. Compound C inhibited ATP synthase. Compound A nearly abolished oxygen consumption and ATP production. Compound B increased oxygen consumption but lowered ATP production. Compound C decreased ATP production and caused oxygen consumption to fall after a brief period.
questions.stem: Which compound is most consistent with an uncoupler?

explanations.C: Correct. Compound B increases oxygen consumption while decreasing ATP production, the classic uncoupling pattern: electron transport accelerates but the proton gradient is dissipated without ATP synthesis.
explanations.A: Compound A blocks Complex IV, reducing oxygen consumption.
explanations.B: Compound C blocks ATP synthase and secondarily slows respiration.
explanations.D: The passage explicitly describes Compound B as proton-carrying.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Relative oxygen consumption and ATP production in isolated mitochondria treated with three compounds.

questions.stem: Why does blocking Complex IV nearly abolish oxygen consumption?

choices.A: Complex IV transfers electrons to oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor
choices.C: Complex IV pumps ATP into the matrix
choices.D: Complex IV oxidizes pyruvate to acetyl-CoA

explanations.A: Correct. Complex IV reduces oxygen to water at the end of the electron transport chain. If it is blocked, electron flow and oxygen consumption collapse.
explanations.B: Glucose phosphorylation occurs in glycolysis, not Complex IV.
explanations.C: ATP is synthesized by ATP synthase, not pumped by Complex IV.
explanations.D: Pyruvate dehydrogenase oxidizes pyruvate to acetyl-CoA.

questions.stem: Why can oxygen consumption initially continue when ATP synthase is inhibited?

choices.D: The proton gradient can rise temporarily before backpressure slows electron transport
choices.A: ATP synthase is the terminal electron acceptor
choices.B: Protons no longer exist in the intermembrane space

explanations.D: Correct. When ATP synthase is blocked, protons accumulate outside the matrix. As the gradient becomes too steep, electron transport slows because further proton pumping becomes unfavorable.
explanations.A: Oxygen, not ATP synthase, is the terminal electron acceptor.
explanations.B: Protons remain central to the gradient.
explanations.C: The passage does not indicate that Complex I becomes an uncoupler.

questions.stem: Which treatment would most directly decrease the proton gradient while allowing electron transport to continue?

explanations.B: Correct. An uncoupler dissipates the proton gradient by allowing protons to re-enter the matrix independently of ATP synthase while electron transport continues.
explanations.A: Blocking Complex IV stops electron transport.
explanations.C: ATP synthase inhibition increases the gradient before slowing respiration.
explanations.D: A cyanide-like inhibitor would block Complex IV, not dissipate the gradient while continuing electron flow.

questions.stem: Which cellular process would be most immediately impaired by all three compounds?

explanations.C: Correct. All three treatments lower mitochondrial ATP production, so oxidative phosphorylation is directly impaired. Other cellular processes may be affected later through low ATP, but they are not the direct shared target.
explanations.A: DNA replication could suffer secondarily but is not the direct pathway measured.
explanations.B: Translation termination is not the shared mitochondrial process.
explanations.D: Glycogen branching is not directly measured or targeted.

questions.passage: A muscle physiology lab compared resting muscle cells with cells undergoing intense contraction under low oxygen. Under low oxygen, glucose consumption increased, lactate release increased, and cytosolic NAD+ levels were preserved. When lactate dehydrogenase was inhibited, glycolytic ATP production rapidly fell despite abundant glucose. The instructor emphasized that fermentation does not make much ATP directly, but it allows glycolysis to continue by regenerating NAD+.
questions.stem: Why does lactate production rise under low oxygen?

choices.D: It regenerates NAD+ so glycolysis can continue
choices.A: It directly produces most mitochondrial ATP
choices.C: It eliminates the need for glycolysis

explanations.D: Correct. Lactate dehydrogenase reduces pyruvate to lactate while oxidizing NADH to NAD+, preserving the NAD+ needed for glycolysis.
explanations.A: Lactate fermentation supports glycolysis, not mitochondrial ATP production.
explanations.B: Acetyl-CoA is not converted into glucose by lactate production.
explanations.C: The purpose is to sustain glycolysis, not replace it.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Relative glucose use, lactate release, and glycolytic ATP production under resting and low-oxygen conditions.

questions.stem: Why does LDH inhibition lower glycolytic ATP under low oxygen?

choices.A: NAD+ regeneration is impaired, limiting glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase
choices.B: Glucose transport becomes impossible in all cells
choices.D: Pyruvate is converted directly to carbon dioxide in the cytosol

explanations.A: Correct. Without LDH, NADH is not efficiently reoxidized to NAD+. Glycolysis slows because NAD+ is required at the glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase step.
explanations.B: The passage does not say glucose transport is blocked.
explanations.C: ATP synthase is mitochondrial and not overactive in low oxygen.
explanations.D: Pyruvate oxidation to CO2 occurs in mitochondria under aerobic conditions, not directly in cytosol fermentation.

questions.stem: Which pathway directly produces ATP during anaerobic glycolysis?

explanations.B: Correct. Glycolysis produces ATP directly through substrate-level phosphorylation reactions. Under low oxygen, this becomes especially important.
explanations.A: Oxidative phosphorylation requires an electron transport chain and oxygen as terminal acceptor.
explanations.C: Beta oxidation produces reducing equivalents but is not anaerobic glycolytic ATP production.
explanations.D: The urea cycle disposes of nitrogen and does not produce glycolytic ATP.

questions.stem: Which finding in Figure 2 best supports increased anaerobic glycolysis?

choices.C: Low oxygen increases glucose use and lactate release together
choices.A: Resting cells have the highest lactate release
choices.D: Low oxygen eliminates all ATP production

explanations.C: Correct. Anaerobic glycolysis consumes more glucose and produces more lactate. The low-oxygen condition shows both variables rising.
explanations.A: Resting cells have low lactate release.
explanations.D: Low oxygen reduces mitochondrial ATP but glycolytic ATP remains elevated relative to rest.

questions.stem: Which molecule would accumulate most directly if LDH is inhibited under low oxygen?

explanations.A: Correct. LDH normally oxidizes NADH to NAD+ while reducing pyruvate. Blocking LDH would cause NADH to accumulate and NAD+ regeneration to fall.
explanations.B: NAD+ would be depleted rather than accumulated.
explanations.C: Oxygen is low by condition and is not produced by LDH.
explanations.D: CO2 is not the direct product of lactate fermentation.
